text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 14 September 2024 

Dr Jonathan Reid  
St. Bartholomew's Hospital  
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE  

Dear Dr Jonathan Reid,  

Michael O'Connor, DOB: 14-11-1987, Hospital No: H9283746 NHS No. 7482019365  
Flat 12, 28 Fleet Street, London, EC4Y 1AA  

Diagnoses: 

Seizure disorder under investigation. 

I conducted a scheduled telephone review today given his frequent business travel and difficulty attending in person across time zones. He reports highly irregular sleep patterns with multiple long-haul flights each month, often working late into the night. His first seizure occurred in May 2024 in Ireland, at night while asleep. He woke with rhythmic twitching of the right arm and a sense of déjà vu. The second and third event was in September 2024 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms. He denies preceding illness, alcohol excess, or new medications around those times. There has been no clear daytime witnessed convulsive activity. He notes intermittent jet lag, sleep deprivation, and stress surrounding each episode. 

Neurological review of systems by phone was otherwise unremarkable: no persistent focal weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss reported. No history of head injury. Family history is non-contributory. He does not drive currently and has self-suspended driving in line with advice already given during triage. 

Investigations and plan: 
- MRI brain with epilepsy protocol has been requested; awaiting scheduling around his travel commitments.  
- Prolonged ambulatory EEG (48–72 hours) to capture sleep-related events; to be arranged with courier electrode kit and remote technical support given his travel pattern (Ã‚Â· remote set-up guide provided).  
- Routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs, calcium, magnesium, glucose, B12, folate, TSH) to exclude metabolic precipitants.  
- Seizure first aid and safety counselled, including avoidance of heights, swimming alone, and power tools; advised to prioritise sleep hygiene when crossing time zones (ï¿½ we discussed a graded light exposure plan and limiting red-eye flights where feasible).  
- No daily antiseizure medication initiated at this stage pending further characterisation; rescue plan not indicated currently as episodes are nocturnal and self-limited, but he will seek urgent care if duration exceeds 5 minutes or clusters occur (â€ warning advice provided). 

We also discussed practical measures for frequent flyers: maintaining a consistent pre-sleep routine, minimising alcohol and caffeine before overnight flights, and booking arrival days that allow restorative sleep. He will keep a detailed seizure diary noting local time zone, flight numbers, sleep duration, and any aura features such as déjà vu or focal limb jerks. 

Follow-up: 
We will review results by telephone in 6–8 weeks, or sooner if further events occur. He agrees to contact the clinic via the secure messaging line if he experiences additional episodes, prolonged confusion, or any daytime events. Travel letter for airlines and insurers has been drafted and will be emailed separately. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes three seizure events: first in May 2024 (at night), and the second and third in September 2024 (during sleep). No other quantified frequency is provided. We need the current seizure frequency over a time frame. The explicit count is 3 events over the period May 2024 through September 2024. That spans roughly 4 months (May to September inclusive). Therefore, the best normalized rate is 3 per 4 months. There is no evidence of a 6-month seizure-free period. No other frequencies are stated.
